current_date at query runtime (server date)."]},"attempt":2,"maxAttempts":4,"previousAttempt":{"sql":"WITH computed AS (
  SELECT
    sub.id AS id,
    sub.age AS age,
    CASE
      WHEN sub.age < 18 THEN '<18'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 18 AND 20 THEN '18-20'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 21 AND 24 THEN '21-24'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 25 AND 29 THEN '25-29'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 30 AND 39 THEN '30-39'
      ELSE '40+'
    END AS age_group
  FROM (
    SELECT s.id, EXTRACT(year FROM age(current_date, s.date_of_birth))::int AS age
    FROM public.students s
    WHERE s.date_of_birth IS NOT NULL
  ) sub
)
SELECT
  c.age_group AS age_group,
  c.age AS age,
  COUNT(c.id) AS student_count
FROM computed c
GROUP BY c.age_group, c.age
ORDER BY
  CASE c.age_group
    WHEN '<18' THEN 1
    WHEN '18-20' THEN 2
    WHEN '21-24' THEN 3
    WHEN '25-29' THEN 4
    WHEN '30-39' THEN 5
    ELSE 6
  END,
  c.age;","validationIssues":["The SQL groups by both age_group and age, producing per-age rows; the visualization then sums student_count by age_group — this is correct but grouping by age only in SQL would be simpler if you only want group totals.","The query returns an age column that the chart does not use. If you intended a per-year distribution, use age as the x-axis instead of age_group.","Tooltip and y-encoding both apply aggregate: sum(student_count). resultRaw.sql:
WITH computed AS (
  SELECT
    sub.id AS id,
    sub.age AS age,
    CASE
      WHEN sub.age < 18 THEN '<18'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 18 AND 20 THEN '18-20'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 21 AND 24 THEN '21-24'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 25 AND 29 THEN '25-29'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 30 AND 39 THEN '30-39'
      ELSE '40+'
    END AS age_group
  FROM (
    SELECT s.id, EXTRACT(year FROM age(current_date, s.date_of_birth))::int AS age
    FROM public.students s
    WHERE s.date_of_birth IS NOT NULL
  ) sub
)
SELECT
  c.age_group AS age_group,
  c.age AS age,
  COUNT(c.id) AS student_count
FROM computed c
GROUP BY c.age_group, c.age
ORDER BY
  CASE c.age_group
    WHEN '<18' THEN 1
    WHEN '18-20' THEN 2
    WHEN '21-24' THEN 3
    WHEN '25-29' THEN 4
    WHEN '30-39' THEN 5
    ELSE 6
  END,
  c.age;

issues: The SQL groups by both age_group and age, producing per-age rows; the visualization then sums student_count by age_group — this is correct but grouping by age only in SQL would be simpler if you only want group totals.
The query returns an age column that the chart does not use. If you intended a per-year distribution, use age as the x-axis instead of age_group.

Tooltip and y-encoding both apply aggregate: sum(student_count). This is appropriate given the SQL produces per-age counts, but be aware the visualization performs an additional aggregation step rather than relying on a single-level aggregation in SQL.

result.sql:
WITH computed AS (
  SELECT
    sub.id AS id,
    sub.age AS age,
    CASE
      WHEN sub.age < 18 THEN '<18'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 18 AND 20 THEN '18-20'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 21 AND 24 THEN '21-24'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 25 AND 29 THEN '25-29'
      WHEN sub.age BETWEEN 30 AND 39 THEN '30-39'
      ELSE '40+'
    END AS age_group
  FROM (
    SELECT s.id, EXTRACT(year FROM age(current_date, s.date_of_birth))::int AS age
    FROM public.students s
    WHERE s.date_of_birth IS NOT NULL
  ) sub
)
SELECT
  c.age_group AS age_group,
  c.age AS age,
  COUNT(c.id) AS student_count
FROM computed c
GROUP BY c.age_group, c.age
ORDER BY
  CASE c.age_group
    WHEN '<18' THEN 1
    WHEN '18-20' THEN 2
    WHEN '21-24' THEN 3
    WHEN '25-29' THEN 4
    WHEN '30-39' THEN 5
    ELSE 6
  END,
  c.age;

issues: The SQL groups by both age_group and age, producing per-age rows; the visualization then sums student_count by age_group — this is correct but grouping by age only in SQL would be simpler if you only want group totals.
The query returns an age column that the chart does not use. If you intended a per-year distribution, use age as the x-axis instead of age_group.

Tooltip and y-encoding both apply aggregate: sum(student_count). This is appropriate given the SQL produces per-age counts, but be aware the visualization performs an additional aggregation step rather than relying on a single-level aggregation in SQL.
